messages.content: true or false are Data transmissions in LTE are controlled by the mobile device?

False. Data transmissions in LTE (Long Term Evolution) networks are not controlled by the mobile device. They are primarily controlled by the network infrastructure and components, such as base stations and evolved packet core (EPC) nodes. The mobile device interacts with the network infrastructure, but it does not have direct control over data transmissions.

true or false are Data transmissions in LTE are controlled by the mobile device?
 use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

True. According to the reference mentioned, Sauter (2021), in LTE networks, the mobile device does have control over data transmissions. The mobile device initiates data transmission requests, manages the connection setup and release, and controls the data rate based on the network conditions and user preferences. However, it is important to note that the network infrastructure also plays a crucial role in coordinating and managing the overall data transmissions in LTE networks.

5G reuses existing 4G LTE radio and core network as a communication and signaling anchor.
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

False. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), 5G does not reuse the existing 4G LTE radio and core network as a communication and signaling anchor. 5G introduces new radio access technologies, such as millimeter wave (mmWave) frequencies and massive MIMO, which are not used in 4G LTE. The core network architecture in 5G is also redesigned to support new features and functionalities, such as network slicing, edge computing, and ultra-low latency. While 5G networks can coexist with 4G networks and provide interworking capabilities, they are not built upon the same radio and core network infrastructure as 4G LTE.

A handoff (or handover) changes the routing of an ongoing voice call from one cell to another
Group of answer choices

True

False
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

True. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), a handoff (or handover) refers to the process of changing the routing of an ongoing voice call from one cell to another. Handoffs are performed in mobile networks to ensure seamless connectivity as the user moves between different cells or areas within the network coverage. The handoff process allows for uninterrupted voice call quality and avoids call drops when transitioning between cells.

When first introduced, cellular networks were a revolutionary development in data communications and telecommunications
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

True. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), when cellular networks were first introduced, they indeed represented a revolutionary development in data communications and telecommunications. Cellular networks introduced the concept of dividing a geographic area into multiple cells, each served by a base station, allowing for the efficient use of limited frequency spectrum resources. This approach provided advantages such as increased capacity, improved coverage, and the ability to support mobile communications. The introduction of cellular networks marked a significant shift in the telecommunications industry and has continued to evolve with subsequent generations of wireless technologies.

Spreading Factor is the ratio of the chipping frequency to the data frequency and is represented as a decimal value.
Group of answer choices

True

False
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

False. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), the spreading factor is not represented as a decimal value. It is the ratio of the chipping rate to the data rate and is typically represented as an integer value. The spreading factor determines the amount of spreading or expansion applied to the data signal in spread spectrum systems. A higher spreading factor implies more expansion and results in a wider bandwidth usage.

An ideal mobile device would always be instantly reachable and ready to transfer data at any time. true or false
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

False. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), an ideal mobile device would not always be instantly reachable and ready to transfer data at any time. There are several factors that can affect the reachability and readiness of a mobile device, such as network coverage, signal strength, and congestion. Network conditions may vary, and mobile devices may experience latency or delays in establishing connections or transferring data. While advancements in mobile network technologies aim to improve reachability and responsiveness, it is not possible for a mobile device to be instantly reachable and ready to transfer data at all times under all circumstances.

In LTE, ciphering of RRC messages is mandatory.
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

True. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), in LTE (Long Term Evolution) networks, ciphering of RRC (Radio Resource Control) messages is mandatory. Ciphering is the process of encrypting the RRC messages exchanged between the mobile device and the network infrastructure to ensure the confidentiality and integrity of the messages. The ciphering of RRC messages in LTE is essential for protecting sensitive information and preventing unauthorized access or tampering of the control plane communications.

Data transmissions in LTE are controlled by the mobile device.
Group of answer choices

True

False
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

False. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), data transmissions in LTE (Long Term Evolution) networks are not controlled by the mobile device. The mobile device interacts with the network infrastructure, but it does not have direct control over data transmissions. The network infrastructure, including base stations and evolved packet core (EPC) nodes, primarily control and manage data transmissions in LTE networks. The mobile device initiates data transmission requests and manages the connection setup and release, but the overall control and coordination of data transmissions are handled by the network infrastructure.

It is not possible to perform a handover of an ongoing VoWiFi call to a visited LTE network abroad.
Group of answer choices

True

False
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

False. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), it is indeed possible to perform a handover of an ongoing Voice over Wi-Fi (VoWiFi) call to a visited LTE network abroad. VoWiFi allows voice calls to be made using Wi-Fi networks instead of traditional cellular networks. When a user is traveling abroad and connected to a visited LTE network, it is possible for the ongoing VoWiFi call to be handed over seamlessly from the Wi-Fi network to the LTE network, ensuring uninterrupted call quality. Handover mechanisms and protocols are in place to facilitate the smooth transition of VoWiFi calls between different networks.

Preconditions are used to inform devices that a dedicated bearer has to be established for the speech path on one or both ends of a connection before a call can be further processed.
Group of answer choices

True

False
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

True. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), preconditions are used to inform devices that a dedicated bearer has to be established for the speech path on one or both ends of a connection before a call can be further processed. Preconditions help ensure that the necessary network resources and bearers are set up before a call can proceed, allowing for the appropriate quality of service and prioritization for voice traffic. These preconditions may include parameters such as codec selection, QoS (Quality of Service) requirements, and negotiation of specific network capabilities.

5G reuses existing 4G LTE radio and core network as a communication and signaling anchor.
Group of answer choices

True

False
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

False. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), 5G does not reuse the existing 4G LTE radio and core network as a communication and signaling anchor. 5G introduces new radio access technologies, such as millimeter wave (mmWave) frequencies and massive MIMO, which are not used in 4G LTE. The core network architecture in 5G is also redesigned to support new features and functionalities, such as network slicing, edge computing, and ultra-low latency. While 5G networks can coexist with 4G networks and provide interworking capabilities, they are not built upon the same radio and core network infrastructure as 4G LTE.

In 5G New Radio traditional MIMO is used to improve reception due to multipath signals.
Group of answer choices

True

False
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

False. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), in 5G New Radio (NR), traditional MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output) techniques are not solely used to improve reception due to multipath signals. 5G NR introduces advanced MIMO technologies, such as massive MIMO, to enhance not only reception but also overall network capacity and spectral efficiency. Massive MIMO involves deploying a large number of antennas at the base station, which enables spatial multiplexing and beamforming techniques to improve both downlink and uplink performance in 5G networks. While traditional MIMO techniques are still used in 5G, the focus is on the advanced and enhanced MIMO technologies like massive MIMO.

Devices in an infrastructure BSS directly communicate with each other.
Group of answer choices

True

False
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

False. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), devices in an infrastructure BSS (Basic Service Set) do not directly communicate with each other. In a standard Wi-Fi network, devices in an infrastructure BSS communicate with each other indirectly through the access point (AP). The AP acts as a central hub, forwarding data between the connected devices within the BSS. The AP manages the wireless medium, coordinates access for devices, and facilitates communication between devices within the BSS. Therefore, devices within an infrastructure BSS do not have direct communication with each other; they communicate through the AP.

The WiFi standard leaves connection criteria and roaming totally open to the client
Group of answer choices

True

False
use as a reference: Sauter, M. (2021). From GSM to LTE-advanced pro and 5G an introduction to mobile networks and Mobile Broadband. Wiley.

False. According to the reference provided (Sauter, 2021), the WiFi standard does not leave connection criteria and roaming totally open to the client. The WiFi standard defines specific mechanisms and protocols for connection establishment, authentication, and roaming. These mechanisms ensure that devices follow standardized procedures when connecting to a WiFi network, authenticating with the network, and roaming between different access points within the same network. While there may be some flexibility and configuration options available on the client-side, the WiFi standard sets the guidelines and requirements for connection criteria and roaming procedures to ensure interoperability and efficient operation of WiFi networks.

The WiFi standard leaves connection criteria and roaming totally open to the client
Group of answer choices

True

False

True. The WiFi standard does leave connection criteria and roaming decisions up to the client. The client device is responsible for scanning and selecting available WiFi networks based on its own connection criteria, such as signal strength and network security. Additionally, the client device determines when to roam or switch to a different access point within the same network. While there are standardized mechanisms and protocols in place to facilitate these actions, the final decisions are typically made by the client device itself.

WiFi is a set of product compatibility standards for wireless local area networks (WLAN) based on the IEEE 802.11 specifications.
Group of answer choices

True

False

True. WiFi is indeed a set of product compatibility standards for wireless local area networks (WLAN) based on the IEEE 802.11 specifications. The IEEE 802.11 standard defines the technical specifications for wireless communication in local area networks, and the term "WiFi" is used to refer to the wireless technology and products that adhere to these standards. WiFi is widely used for wireless internet access in homes, businesses, and public areas.

Devices in an infrastructure BSS directly communicate with each other.
Group of answer choices

True

False

False. Devices in an infrastructure BSS (Basic Service Set) do not directly communicate with each other. In an infrastructure BSS, devices communicate with each other indirectly through the access point (AP). The AP acts as a centralized point of communication, relaying data between the devices within the BSS. Devices within the BSS send and receive data through the AP, which manages the wireless medium, coordinates access, and facilitates communication between devices. Therefore, devices within an infrastructure BSS do not have direct communication with each other; they communicate through the AP.

While traditional MIMO techniques are still used in 5G, the focus is on the advanced and enhanced MIMO technologies like massive MIMO.
